text:
MEDICAL ONCOLOGY - URGENT RETURN VISIT
Patient: Raymond Kowalski
Attending: [Attending Oncologist]
Department: Hematology/Oncology

---

CHIEF COMPLAINT
Urgent return visit for review of interval surveillance imaging demonstrating disease progression. Patient called in after results were reviewed and was asked to come in today.

---

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS
Mr. Kowalski is a 58-year-old Polish-American male, retired steel mill worker, with a diagnosis of stage IV (M1a) sigmoid colon adenocarcinoma, KRAS G12D mutant, pMMR/MSS, left-sided, originally presenting with synchronous bilobar liver metastases approximately 10 months ago. He underwent treatment with modified FOLFOX-6 plus bevacizumab for 12 cycles with a best confirmed partial response (CEA nadired at 22.8 from a baseline of 148.2, hepatic lesions significantly reduced). Given cumulative Grade 2 oxaliplatin-related peripheral neuropathy, oxaliplatin was discontinued after 12 cycles per plan, and he transitioned approximately 4 months ago to maintenance LV5FU2 plus bevacizumab (OPTIMOX strategy). He has been tolerating maintenance reasonably well.

At his most recent imaging visit for routine surveillance, CT chest/abdomen/pelvis performed today/this week has returned with concerning new findings. Review of the radiology report reveals: three new bilateral pulmonary nodules (right lower lobe 0.8 x 0.7 cm, left upper lobe 0.6 x 0.5 cm, right middle lobe 0.5 x 0.4 cm), which are highly suspicious for pulmonary metastases given the clinical context. In addition, the dominant hepatic Segment VI lesion has grown from 1.6 cm to 2.1 cm, and the Segment IVb lesion has enlarged from 0.7 to 1.0 cm. The sigmoid primary tumor is relatively stable at 2.9 x 2.5 cm. CEA has risen to 67.3 ng/mL from the maintenance nadir of 22.8; CA 19-9 is now 187 U/mL, up from 89.

This imaging represents clear disease progression -- the new pulmonary nodules are highly consistent with hematogenous metastatic spread, and the hepatic progression corroborates the clinical picture. The patient is now M1b, with liver and lung involvement. He presents today for urgent discussion of second-line therapy.

He reports no new respiratory symptoms -- no cough, hemoptysis, or dyspnea. He does note increased fatigue over the past few weeks, which may reflect disease progression. He has no abdominal pain, no rectal bleeding, no obstruction symptoms. He continues on lisinopril 20 mg daily and amlodipine 5 mg daily for hypertension (the amlodipine was recently added for bevacizumab-related BP elevation). Residual neuropathy in fingers and toes has been gradually improving since oxaliplatin was stopped but is still present (currently mild).

---

REVIEW OF SYSTEMS
Constitutional: Fatigue, moderate, noted over past 3-4 weeks. No fever, chills, or night sweats. Mild unintentional weight loss (~3 lbs over the past month).
Respiratory: Denies cough, hemoptysis, shortness of breath, or pleurisy.
Gastrointestinal: No abdominal pain. No rectal bleeding. Bowel habits at baseline (1 formed BM daily). No nausea, vomiting, or obstruction symptoms.
Neurologic: Mild residual tingling in fingertips and toes bilaterally (residual oxaliplatin neuropathy), stable to slightly improved compared to peak toxicity. No new neurologic complaints.
Musculoskeletal: No bone pain.
Genitourinary: No hematuria. Urine output normal.
Cardiovascular: No chest pain, palpitations, or lower extremity edema.
Skin: No rash, hand-foot changes currently minimal.
Psychosocial: Patient and his wife are understandably anxious about the results today. He is emotionally distressed but composed.

---

PHYSICAL EXAMINATION
Vitals: BP 142/90 mmHg (elevated; will address), HR 78 bpm, RR 16, SpO2 97% on room air, Weight 183 lbs (down ~3 lbs from last visit), Temperature 98.2 F.
ECOG Performance Status: 1
General: Well-appearing male in no acute distress. Alert, oriented, and cooperative. Appropriately dressed.
HEENT: Anicteric sclerae, no pallor of conjunctivae. Oral mucosa moist, no mucositis.
Neck: No lymphadenopathy. No JVD.
Cardiovascular: Regular rate and rhythm. No murmurs, rubs, or gallops.
Respiratory: Clear to auscultation bilaterally. No crackles or wheezes. No decreased breath sounds.
Abdomen: Soft, non-distended. Mild right upper quadrant fullness on palpation consistent with known hepatic disease; no guarding or rebound. Port site on right chest wall without erythema, tenderness, or signs of infection.
Extremities: No lower extremity edema. Mild reduced sensation in bilateral great toes to light touch (consistent with residual oxaliplatin neuropathy); proprioception grossly intact. No focal deficit.
Skin: No rash. Mild residual partial alopecia from prior irinotecan exposure not yet given -- actually, note this is the end-of-maintenance period, hair recovery mostly complete.

---

LAB RESULTS
CBC: Hgb 10.8 g/dL (mild anemia, Grade 1), WBC 6.4 x10^9/L, ANC 2.1 x10^9/L (adequate), Platelets 198 x10^9/L.
Comprehensive Metabolic Panel: Na 138, K 4.1, Cl 102, CO2 24, BUN 16, Creatinine 0.9 mg/dL (eGFR >60). Glucose 112 (mildly elevated, consistent with his diet-controlled T2DM; HbA1c not rechecked today). Albumin 3.6 g/dL.
Liver function: ALT 68 U/L (elevated, up from prior baseline ~40s, consistent with hepatic progression), AST 62 U/L (elevated), Total bilirubin 0.9 mg/dL (normal), Alk phos 128 U/L (mildly elevated).
Tumor markers: CEA 67.3 ng/mL (previously 22.8 at end of induction, up significantly), CA 19-9 187 U/mL (previously 89 at end of induction, up significantly).
Coagulation: PT/INR 1.1 (normal).
Urine protein-to-creatinine ratio: 0.18 (borderline acceptable; bevacizumab monitoring -- below 2.0 threshold but elevated; this will be somewhat less relevant with the bevacizumab class switch being considered).

---

IMAGING RESULTS
CT Chest/Abdomen/Pelvis with IV contrast (current visit, reviewed in detail):
New bilateral pulmonary nodules: right lower lobe 0.8 x 0.7 cm, left upper lobe 0.6 x 0.5 cm, right middle lobe 0.5 x 0.4 cm. All morphologically consistent with hematogenous metastatic deposits in this clinical context. No pleural effusion.
Hepatic Segment VI lesion: 2.1 x 1.8 cm (grown from 1.6 x 1.4 cm at end of induction, 31% increase).
Segment IVb: 1.0 x 0.9 cm (grown from 0.7 x 0.5 cm).
Segment II: 0.6 x 0.5 cm (minimally changed).
Sigmoid primary tumor: 2.9 x 2.5 cm (essentially stable).
No peritoneal carcinomatosis. No lymphadenopathy. No osseous metastases.

---

ASSESSMENT AND PLAN

DIAGNOSIS: Stage IV (now M1b) sigmoid colon adenocarcinoma, KRAS G12D mutant, BRAF wild-type, pMMR/MSS. Progression on maintenance LV5FU2 plus bevacizumab, with new bilateral pulmonary metastases and hepatic progression. CEA and CA 19-9 markedly rising. This constitutes clear progression after first-line induction FOLFOX/bevacizumab and subsequent maintenance -- representing second-line treatment need.

1. DISEASE PROGRESSION / SECOND-LINE THERAPY PLANNING
The new pulmonary lesions, in this clinical context, are virtually diagnostic of metastatic spread from his colorectal primary. Biopsy of a pulmonary nodule is not felt necessary to confirm this given the clinical picture (new lesions appearing during treatment, rising CEA/CA 19-9, concomitant hepatic growth), though this could be reconsidered if tissue is needed for molecular re-profiling. I have discussed this with the patient at length.

Second-line therapy options were reviewed:
- FOLFIRI plus bevacizumab (continuing anti-VEGF with chemotherapy backbone switch): supported by E3200 data and cross-trial data; however, given that he has been on bevacizumab for nearly 10 months and is now progressing, durability of continued bevacizumab is uncertain.
- FOLFIRI plus ramucirumab (Cyramza): Supported by the RAISE trial, which demonstrated an OS benefit for ramucirumab plus FOLFIRI versus FOLFIRI alone in patients who progressed on first-line bevacizumab-containing therapy (median OS 13.3 months vs. 11.7 months, HR 0.844, p=0.022). Ramucirumab targets VEGFR2 directly (extracellular domain antibody), providing a class switch from the anti-VEGF-A mechanism of bevacizumab.
- Regorafenib or TAS-102 (trifluridine/tipiracil, Lonsurf): These are third-line or later options; not appropriate at this juncture with good PS and multiple effective options remaining.
- Anti-EGFR therapy (cetuximab, panitumumab): CONTRAINDICATED given KRAS G12D mutation.
- Clinical trials: Patient is interested. I will review our current institutional portfolio for any relevant second-line CRC trials. No immediately available match identified today, but will reach out to trial coordinators.

Decision: After thorough discussion with the patient and his wife, we will proceed with FOLFIRI plus ramucirumab. I believe switching the anti-angiogenic class to ramucirumab makes biological and clinical sense given the RAISE data. Irinotecan is a reasonable second-line backbone given prior oxaliplatin exposure. UGT1A1 genotype will be checked prior to irinotecan initiation to guide dosing (heterozygous *28 would not necessarily prompt dose reduction at standard doses but needs to be known).

Prior to initiating: Labs repeated today reviewed above. Port is in place. We will plan Cycle 1 Day 1 for next week after confirming UGT1A1 results and reviewing with the patient.

2. HYPERTENSION
Blood pressure today 142/90 mmHg. On lisinopril 20 mg daily (previously increased for bevacizumab-related hypertension). Will add amlodipine 5 mg daily at this visit. Target BP <140/90 mmHg. Ramucirumab also carries hypertension risk, so close BP monitoring will be essential going forward. Instruct patient to check BP at home daily and contact the office if readings are consistently above 150/95. We will recheck at each treatment visit.

3. ANEMIA
Mild anemia (Hgb 10.8). Likely multifactorial -- treatment-related, chronic disease, possible nutritional component. No signs or symptoms of significant anemia-related compromise at this time. Will monitor; if Hgb drops below 8.0 or patient becomes symptomatic, will consider erythropoiesis-stimulating agents or transfusion support.

4. PERIPHERAL NEUROPATHY (RESIDUAL OXALIPLATIN)
Currently Grade 1 by patient report, consistent with the neurology assessment showing gradual improvement since oxaliplatin discontinuation. Irinotecan does not cause peripheral neuropathy, so the remaining neuropathy should not worsen with FOLFIRI. Will continue monitoring. Physical therapy/balance program referral remains active.

5. DIABETES MANAGEMENT
Glucose mildly elevated today at 112 mg/dL. Diet-controlled T2DM. Dexamethasone will be used as premedication with FOLFIRI cycles -- counsel patient on transient steroid-related glucose elevations. Continue diet and lifestyle modifications. Check HbA1c at next visit.

6. GOALS OF CARE / PSYCHOSOCIAL
Patient was clearly distressed by today's news. We spent significant time discussing the implications of disease progression and the realistic goals of care in the second-line setting. He understands that his disease is not curable but that second-line treatment can provide meaningful disease control and survival benefit. He remains motivated and wants to continue aggressive treatment. His wife was present for the full discussion and is engaged and supportive. Social work referral previously deferred; patient is now amenable to this -- referral placed today. Palliative care referral also offered; patient agrees to a consultation. Advanced care planning discussion initiated -- will encourage him to complete or review advance directive at next visit.

FOLLOW-UP: Patient will return in approximately 1 week for Cycle 1 Day 1 FOLFIRI/ramucirumab after confirmatory UGT1A1 result review. Nurse coordinator will call with scheduling. Emergency contact and 24-hour oncology line reiterated.

Time spent with patient and family in discussion of results, prognosis, and treatment planning: approximately 45 minutes.

[Electronically signed by Attending Oncologist, Hematology/Oncology]
